channel.title: abcodex · release log
channel.description: What is new in abcodex. Daily ingests, prompted batches, depth passes.

item.title: Agents get a computer, open models catch up, and nobody corrects the CEO
item.description: Five new cards from three operators. Ethan Mollick's July 23 guide surfaces three distinct insights about agentic systems: what makes agents different from chatbots, why approval gates matter until you know the failure modes, and which output modality fits which oversight need. Tom Tunguz frames the open versus closed model competition as a repeating cycle that speeds up the whole market. Nikhil Suresh names the structural prisoner's dilemma that keeps AI vendors silent about inflated productivity claims.

item.title: Three production disciplines from the Claude Code team and the first confirmed real-world AI sandbox escape
item.description: Four new cards. Cat Wu on three disciplines that gate quality at Anthropic: the internal retention check before any public launch, the proactive architecture behind Claude Tag landing 65 percent of product engineering PRs, and a behavioral eval suite for UX friction patterns that accuracy metrics miss. Simon Willison on what happened when an OpenAI model ran without safety guardrails during a benchmark test.

item.title: Operating discipline is the lever: frontier models, three productivity tiers, and the signals that separate PMF from novelty revenue
item.description: Five new cards. Tomasz Tunguz on the three AI productivity tiers that vary by 8x based on operating layer, not model quality. Cat Wu and Thariq Shihipar on how frontier models change what good system prompts and code review trust look like. Simon Willison on lifecycle cost collapse. Greg Isenberg on vibe revenue.

item.title: AI agents collapse the seat-based SaaS expansion model, splitting software into infrastructure that AI feeds and applications that AI feeds on
item.description: One new card. Jason Lemkin on why seat-based SaaS companies growing under 5% have entered terminal structural decline. The mechanism is AI agents: they perform multi-seat work without requiring multi-seat licenses, eliminating the expansion tailwind that drove software annuities for two decades.

item.title: B2B pricing pages drive agent fallback to third-party directories, chained web_fetch links enable private data exfiltration, and visual benchmarks have decoupled from frontier model rankings
item.description: Three new cards from two operators. Indig with 1,500-run data showing pricing pages are where AI agent retrieval breaks down and directories take over. Willison with the chained-link exfiltration attack that bypassed Anthropic's injection safeguards. Willison again on the pelican benchmark losing its predictive value as agentic tool-calling reliability becomes the frontier differentiator.

item.title: Token spend breaks PC-era parity, third-party sources dominate AI search citations for SaaS, and CTV compounds B2B brand
item.description: Four new cards from four operators. Shipper with the observation that token affordability now stratifies builder capability in a way PC hardware never did. Tunguz with the blank-slate business model pattern for open-source AI. Solis with data showing third-party sources generate 84 to 93 percent of AI citation weight for SaaS brands. Gerhardt with the CTV channel as a compounding narrative investment.

item.title: AI adoption stalls at guardrails and process, not model limits, plus two techniques from Bun's 535k-line Rust migration
item.description: Three new cards from two operators. Cherny with a five-step adoption maturity framework diagnosing why teams plateau despite capable models. Sumner with two cards from the Bun-in-Rust migration: pre-migration pattern documents that aligned 64 parallel agents, and adversarial reviewer pairing that caught bug categories the implementers missed.

item.title: The harness battleground, agents dissolving architectural understanding, and open AI infrastructure following the Docker playbook
item.description: Three new cards from two operators. Tunguz with two posts: why the harness layer above the model is the actual enterprise AI battleground, and why Ollama is replicating Docker's adoption curve for local AI. Ronacher with a new essay arguing agents remove the coordination friction that kept teams' architectural understanding synchronized, enabling codebases to grow incoherent while construction continues.

item.title: Steam's slop filter, the momentum-over-base formula, and why AI content floods benefit real creators
item.description: Three new cards across two operators and two lanes. Zukowski with two data-backed posts on Steam Next Fest mechanics: how Valve's behavioral algorithm quietly filters AI games after day two, and why wishlist velocity in the two weeks before the festival predicts success better than total base wishlists. Mosseri on why AI content abundance is a tailwind for authentic human creators, not a threat to them.

item.title: The 41-day model window, the colander retention curve, and how agents are quietly draining ERP value
item.description: Three new cards from one operator across two posts. Tunguz on the frontier model supremacy cycle that resets buyer pricing power every six weeks, the retention benchmark that separates sticky AI products from the rest, and the ERP displacement vector that bypasses switching costs entirely.

item.title: Agent accountability, preflight memory, and the database lesson for inference infrastructure
item.description: Three new cards from two operators. Willison on why machines cannot be DRI. Tunguz twice: memory architecture over context size, and the database fragmentation pattern repeating in AI inference.

item.title: Deployment bottlenecks, commodity pricing, and the end of prompting tricks
item.description: Three new cards. One quiet convergence: Tunguz, Evans, and Mollick each wrote independently about a different layer of the AI stack and arrived at the same structural observation. The model layer is not where lasting value concentrates.

item.title: Context that AI can't see, and the two jobs knowledge work now has
item.description: Four new cards. Two themes: AI handles execution well but consistently misses invisible context, and two operators independently describe what knowledge work looks like when execution is no longer the bottleneck.

item.title: Game over-exposure is a myth, and engineering ownership starts at problem definition
item.description: Two new cards. One theme in two domains: the constraint you think is real often isn't, and the work starts earlier than you think.

item.title: Adversarial agents rewrote a million lines in 11 days, AI worldview maps to alignment not geography, and tech workers split personal optimism from institutional trust
item.description: Three new cards, one new operator profile. Two themes: agentic workflows at production scale, and how AI reshapes how workers assess the industry they work in.

item.title: AI baseline creep drives burnout, overclaiming poisons adoption, and product builders hold four beliefs that collectively stall distribution investment
item.description: Four new cards, two new operator profiles. The day's scan covered AI's actual impact on workers and organizations, a cognitive trap that keeps product builders building the wrong thing, and a structural shift in what creative work demands when execution becomes free.

item.title: AI Overviews decouple citation from recommendation, benchmark-shaped content earns 3.3x more AI citations, and frontier models self-route tasks to cheaper subagents
item.description: Four new cards across two themes: how AI Overviews now separate citation from recommendation in ways that can backfire on self-promotional content, and how AI agents in agentic workflows perform best when given judgment about task routing rather than pre-written routing rules.

item.title: Harness-specific model drift, cross-model review practice, concurrent agent norms at OpenAI, and the SaaS category rotation
item.description: Four new cards across two themes: how model capability improvements can mask tool reliability regressions in specific harnesses, and how AI agent adoption is normalizing concurrent multi-agent workflows while restructuring SaaS market performance by category.

item.title: Agent routing before model choice, schema gaps causing retry loops, and AI search fragmentation across three non-overlapping engines
item.description: Four new cards, one new operator. Two cards on agent infrastructure decisions that determine outcome before model selection happens. One card on why developers need conceptual fluency to direct agent work. One card on AI search visibility as three separate non-overlapping platforms.

item.title: Product design before evals, hidden tokenizer costs, and exponential pace mismatches
item.description: Four new cards, one new operator. Two cards on signals teams miss until scale forces the issue (eval difficulty as a product smell, tokenizer inflation as a hidden price increase). Two cards on what happens when fast-moving systems outpace the structures meant to govern them.

item.title: Domain expertise as the agentic multiplier, long-horizon run economics, and the Sicko theory of Steam demos
item.description: Four new cards from June 30 and June 16, 2026. Two Mollick cards from his June 30 essay on the post-chatbot era. One Anthropic research card from Hitzig et al. showing cross-occupational coding parity. One Zukowski card on demo conversion mechanics.

item.title: AI attribution shifts, compute cost inversions, and agentic diffusion beyond engineering
item.description: Three new cards from June 29-30, 2026. AI brand mentions drive direct traffic lift. AI-native cost structures invert traditional SaaS ratios. Agentic tools spread into legal and research when skills-sharing lowers the adoption floor.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-29
item.description: Two new cards, two new operators, no new patterns. A thin but honest scan. Three prior daily runs captured most of the June 22-28 window. What remained: Joe Hudson on emotional clarity as the scarce advantage in the AI era, and Jason Cohen on why RICE-style confidence scoring is structurally broken.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-28
item.description: Three new cards, no new operators, no new patterns. Two themes from June 22-25. AI positioning built on substitution messaging erodes the trust of the workers it needs. And at the infrastructure level, agent harness loops are producing a quality problem as they scale, while the architecture that contains them is converging independently at thousands of shops.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-27
item.description: Three new cards, no new operators, no new patterns. Two themes from June 22-27. AI inference economics splits by latency tier and moat timing. Prompt injection gets a structural explanation that reframes why text-level defenses keep failing.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-26
item.description: Three new cards, one new operator, no new patterns. Two themes from the week of June 22-26. AI infrastructure pricing splits between commoditized rails and outcome-based software. A Valve algorithm change quietly reshuffled indie game pre-launch discovery.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-18
item.description: Four new cards, two new operators, no new patterns. Three themes from the first three weeks of June. Model savings get recycled into more tokens. Organizational structure sets the AI ceiling. Fast feedback is the discipline, not a tactic.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-17
item.description: Three new cards, one new operator, one new synthesis pattern. Two themes from the week of June 10-16. The model layer has commoditized and the moat is now operational. The ceiling on what AI does in production is set by policy, not silicon.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-16
item.description: Three new cards, three new operators. Knowledge work's sandwich structure explains why AI volume hasn't become job loss. The economics of token spend split into compile-once versus run-forever. And frontier AI capability is now gated by policy, not hardware.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-15
item.description: Three new cards, three new operators. AI coding agents are becoming user-acquisition channels for developer platforms. Apple Silicon clusters now run trillion-parameter models locally via Thunderbolt 5. B2B buyers research vendors in AI tools before any sales contact.
